# Lists of pointers

::: callout-tip
## Objective

Build a vector of Rust objects by collecting external pointers into a list.

:::

In the last chapter we made a single external pointer. R users don't want one
value at a time though—they want a **vector**. How do we hold a vector of Rust
objects?

## Lists *are* vectors

Remember that in R a `list` is just a vector—a vector whose elements can be
anything. That's exactly what we need: each element will be an external pointer.

So to return many Rust objects to R, we build a `List` where every element is a
pointer.

## `NULL` is our missing value

A character vector can contain `NA`. When we turn each string into a pointer, we
need somewhere to put "this one is missing." In a list, the natural missing value
is `NULL`. So:

- if the input string is `NA`, the element becomes `NULL`
- if it parses, the element becomes the external pointer
- if it *fails* to parse, the element also becomes `NULL`

We turn any value—a pointer or a `NULL`—into a generic `Robj` so that every
element of the iterator has the same type and can be collected together.

## Example

Here we parse a character vector of shape names into a list of `Shape` objects:

```rust
#[extendr]
fn parse_shapes(x: Strings) -> List {
    x.into_iter()
        .map(|xi| {
            if xi.is_na() {
                return ().into_robj();
            }

            match Shape::new(xi) {
                Ok(shape) => shape.into_robj(),
                Err(_) => ().into_robj(),
            }
        })
        .collect::<List>()
}
```

::: callout-note
## Collecting into wrapper types

Recall that we can `.collect()` directly into `Strings`, `Doubles`, etc. when
every element is the matching scalar type (`Rstr`, `Rfloat`, ...). Here, because
every element is a generic `Robj`, we collect into a `List`.
:::

## Exercise

- Write `url_parse(x: Strings) -> List`
- For each element: `NA` becomes `NULL`, a parsed url becomes an external
  pointer, a parse failure becomes `NULL`
- Use the `RUrl::new()` constructor you wrote in the last chapter
- Collect into a `List`

<details>
    <summary>View solution</summary>

```rust
#[extendr]
fn url_parse(x: Strings) -> List {
    x.into_iter()
        .map(|xi| {
            if xi.is_na() {
                return ().into_robj();
            }

            match RUrl::new(xi) {
                Ok(v) => v.into_robj(),
                Err(_) => ().into_robj(),
            }
        })
        .collect::<List>()
}
```

(We'll come back and give this list a class in the S3 chapter.)

</details>
